content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 3L, L99L
Dan C. Dunmoyer
vice President of Legislative Policy
Personal Insurance Federation of California
801 K Street, suite 1936
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
Re: Your Request For Advice
Our Fil,e No. A-91-459
Dear Mr. Dunmoyer:
You have requested advice on the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the rrActrr).1/
OUESTION
As a reqistered lobbyist, are you required to report on your
quarterly toUUying discfosure report legislative officials 
who
ittended- your weaaing and reception? If you are required to
report thls activity, 
how do you determine the actual reportable
costs of the event?
CONCLUSION
Reportable officials 
who attended your weddinq. reception have
received gifts and are, therefore, required to be disclosed on
your lobbying disclosure report. 
To deternine the arnount of the
gift received by each official, 
divide the total cost of the
ieception by the total number of persons attending. The total
cost of the recepti-on incl-udes pa)rments for food and beverages,
decorations, entertainnent, caterers, and sirnilar expenses.
I/ covernment code Sections 81000-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulatj-ons appear at 2 California Code of Regul-ations Section
18oOO, et seq. AIl references to regulations are to Tit1e 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 . P.O, Box 807 o Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 . (gf6) 32?-5660


Dan C. Dunmoyer
Page 2
ANALYSIS
Lobbyists are reguired to report as activity expenses on
their quarterly lobbying reports gifts and other paynents which
benefit reportable officials. 
Reportable officials include a
state candidate, elected state officer, or legislative official,
an agency official 
of a state agency when the official 
is required
to be listed on the registration statement of the lobbying firm or
the lobbyist employer of the lobbyist. 
(section 86201.) An
activity expense is any palment which benefits in whole or in part
an elective state official, 
a legislative official., an agency
official, 
a state candidate, or a nenber of the innediate fanily
of such an official 
or candidate. Activity expenses include
gifts, honoraria, consultingT fees, salaries, and any other forrn of
compensation but do not include campaign contrj-butions. (Section
86111. )
Section 82028 defines trgiftrr as3
Any payment to the extent that consideration of equal
or greater value is not received and includes a rebate
or discount in the price of anything of value unless
the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to
official 
status....
Section 86203 provides that it is unlawful for a lobbyist,
or lobbying f irrn, to make gifts to one person aggreqating more
than ten dollars in a calendar nonth, or to act as an agent or
intermediary in the nakj.ng of any gift, or to arrange for the
making of any gift by any other person.
rf you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5662.
Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By: Mary Ann Kvasager
PoIiticaI Reform Consultant
